era_context: The Ur III dynasty in Mesopotamia is in its final decades under Ibbi-Sin. Egypt is fragmented in the First Intermediate Period. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its mature phase. Traditional Chinese historiography places the Xia dynasty in its early-to-middle period. The Bronze Age is well-established across Eurasia; long-distance trade networks connect distant regions.

events.title: Ur III state under mounting pressure from external enemies
events.description: The Ur III state under Ibbi-Sin faces intensifying military pressure from Elamite forces to the east and Amorite groups to the west. Cuneiform texts record military campaigns and defensive measures. The state's ability to maintain control over distant provinces is declining. Economic resources are increasingly devoted to military expenditures. The administrative system that had unified Mesopotamia is fragmenting as regional governors assert greater autonomy. The dynasty is in its final years before collapse.

sources.name: Ur III cuneiform administrative and military texts

events.certainty_note: Cuneiform sources are contemporary and abundant. Absolute dating to -2008 relies on conventional Mesopotamian chronology (±50 years). The general trend is clear; the specific year is approximate.

events.title: Harappan civilization: continued urban and economic development
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its mature phase with thriving urban centers and extensive trade networks. Harappan merchants maintain connections with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. Craft production, including pottery, metalwork, and stone carving, is sophisticated and standardized. The civilization shows no signs of major disruption at this time.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan mature phase is well-established archaeologically. -2008 falls within the period c. 2600–1900 BCE with high confidence.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -2008 is sparse and regionally uneven. Mesopotamian cuneiform sources are abundant but administrative and military in nature. The Indus Valley is known only through archaeology. Egypt is known through later sources and archaeology. China is known only through traditional historiography. Large regions of the world are absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Military pressure on Ur III leads to the dynasty's collapse
